## The 5 Most Influential Sources

### 1. `external/2026-05-20-tool-wip-limits-honest-review.md`
**Why it matters:** Definitively answers TWO of the four Command Brief open questions with hands-on 2026 evidence. Confirms Linear has NO native WIP limits (as of April 2026), documents the Jira swimlane WIP count bug (no fix, Atlassian has sat on it for years), and confirms GitHub Projects column limits are visual-only with zero enforcement. This is the single most practically-useful source for `guides/08-tool-implementation.md`. Any team currently using these tools will encounter exactly these issues, and the worker-bee must know them cold.

### 2. `external/2026-05-20-wip-limits-agile-flow-consulting.md`
**Why it matters:** Contains the clearest real-world WIP limit case study in the research run — a 12-person team with a 42% cycle time reduction (p85 from 62 days to 36 days) by implementing throughput-derived WIP limits. Also provides the "current WIP minus one" quickstart heuristic and the explicit Little's Law connection to the WIP-to-cycle-time relationship. Directly illustrates the worker-bee's core promise to clients.

### 3. `external/2026-05-20-flow-metrics-definitions-cylenivo.md`
**Why it matters:** Provides the cleanest, most precise definitions of the four core flow metrics (cycle time, lead time, throughput, WIP) and flow efficiency as a derived metric. The cycle time vs. lead time distinction with explicit start/end clocks is exactly what the worker-bee's critical directive demands. The "15-20% flow efficiency is typical — it's a system problem, not a people problem" framing is the most useful reframe for client coaching.

### 4. `external/2026-05-20-littles-law-abstract-algorithms.md`
**Why it matters:** The most technically thorough 2026 source on Little's Law itself. Provides the three rearrangements (each a diagnostic dial), the cascade effect (WIP bloat from slow-moving items), the steady-state requirement with the 1.5-2× safety factor, and a directly usable application table (including CI/CD pipeline as a row). Directly maps to the Command Brief's "three-variable dial" instruction for `guides/03-littles-law.md`.

### 5. `external/2026-05-20-kanban-vs-scrum-decision-eitt.md`
**Why it matters:** The only source with State of Agile Report 2026 adoption data (94% of IT teams use Agile; Scrum 70%, Kanban 25%, 45% of Scrum teams adopt Scrumban after 2-3 years). Provides a complete three-path decision framework (Scrum for new teams, Kanban for operations, Scrumban for mature teams) and a migration timeline. The adoption statistics are citable benchmarks that the worker-bee can use to validate why a team's current choice may not be optimal.

## Open Questions for stinger-forge to Resolve

### Q1: Linear WIP limit enforcement — RESOLVED
**Answer:** Linear does NOT have native WIP limits as of April 2026. Enforcement requires team conventions or GraphQL API workarounds. The API is solid and the query pattern is documented in `external/2026-05-20-tool-wip-limits-honest-review.md`.

**Action for stinger-forge:** In `guides/08-tool-implementation.md` (Linear section), lead with this limitation and provide three options: (a) soft enforcement via team agreement, (b) naming convention (e.g., "In Progress (max 3)"), (c) GraphQL API automation. Do not present Linear as a full-featured Kanban tool for strict WIP enforcement.

### Q2: Daniel Vacanti / ActionableAgile Monte Carlo for small teams — UNRESOLVED
**Research finding:** No 2025-2026 content from Vacanti, ActionableAgile Analytics, or the broader flow-metrics community was found that specifically addresses Monte Carlo forecasting for small teams (under 5 people). The normal-depth research run did not surface any new guidance in the 6-month window.

**Action for stinger-forge:** Either (a) reference Vacanti's existing Actionable Agile Metrics book (2015) as the primary source (it does cover small samples), or (b) note in `guides/03-littles-law.md` that Monte Carlo with fewer than 15-20 historical data points requires additional statistical caution, and point users to the ActionableAgile Analytics tool (https://actionableagile.com/) for simulation tooling. This is the highest-priority unresolved gap.

### Q3: Kanban vs Shape Up comparison — UNRESOLVED
**Research finding:** No formal comparison of Kanban vs. Shape Up was found in the 6-month research window. Shape Up is primarily documented in Basecamp's own materials (https://basecamp.com/shapeup), which are not actively updated in 2025-2026. The comparison may simply not exist as a formal practitioner article.

**Action for stinger-forge:** Either (a) omit Shape Up from `guides/07-kanban-vs-scrum.md` and note it is out of scope, or (b) author a brief comparative note from first principles — Shape Up uses 6-week appetite-based cycles with explicit no-estimates, while Kanban uses continuous flow with WIP limits and cycle time measurement. The two are not direct alternatives (Shape Up is more like a modified Scrum with a different planning rhythm). This is low-priority given the Command Brief's note that "may not exist but worth checking."

### Q4: GitHub Projects native WIP limits — RESOLVED
**Answer:** GitHub Projects does NOT support native WIP limit enforcement. Column limits are soft visual indicators only (the column header shows current count vs limit in red, but anyone can add cards beyond the limit). This is confirmed by both the official GitHub documentation and a practitioner review.

**Action for stinger-forge:** In `guides/08-tool-implementation.md` (GitHub Projects section), document this limitation clearly, recommend the GitHub Actions workaround for teams that need enforcement (and escalate the implementation to `devops-worker-bee`), and note that teams using GitHub Projects for serious Kanban practice will need a separate analytics tool for flow metrics (e.g., ActionableAgile, Swarmia, or a custom CSV export).

---

## Additional Research Notes for stinger-forge

### Flow efficiency benchmark discrepancy
Two sources cite slightly different flow efficiency averages:
- Cylenivo: "typical software teams land around 15-20%"
- Axify: "in most software delivery environments, flow efficiency falls in the single digits up to roughly 15%"

Both may be accurate for different team populations. Stinger-forge should present a range of 5-20% in `guides/02-flow-metrics.md`, with the note that mature, WIP-limited teams are at the high end and most unoptimized teams are at the low end.

### DORA metrics overlap
Several flow metrics sources (Axify, Zylos, StackFYI) discuss DORA metrics alongside Kanban flow metrics. The worker-bee's domain is Kanban-native flow metrics, not DORA. Stinger-forge should acknowledge the relationship in `guides/02-flow-metrics.md` (lead time for changes is a subset of Kanban lead time; deployment frequency is a throughput proxy) without making DORA metrics the primary measurement system.

### Jira swimlane WIP count bug
The Jira WIP limit swimlane counting issue (counts total per column across all swimlanes, not per swimlane) is a longstanding undocumented limitation that the DEV Community source confirms Atlassian has not resolved. This means teams with swimlanes in Jira will see false WIP limit violations for perfectly healthy boards. Stinger-forge should flag this as a critical gotcha in `guides/08-tool-implementation.md` and recommend either flat boards (no swimlanes) when WIP accuracy is required, or using label-based visual organization instead of Jira swimlanes.
